text: We performed a staging mediastinoscopy and following this the patient was prepped and draped in a usual manner for a left robotic approach.
And we incorporated a number of the previous port sites in order to establish robotic access.
And the robot was brought in and docked.
Here we can see most of the mediastinum was already dissected and we're using a double fenestrated grasper to dissect out the main pulmonary artery.
Of course, being very careful to protect the left recurrent laryngeal nerve from any damage.
And cautery can be used in this area, although with caution.
Both the entry and exit points are developed and the appropriate angle for stapling is determined.
Here you can see the left main pulmonary artery being successfully stapled with a standard robotic vascular stapler.
And as you can see, there is adequate hemostasis.
Next, our attention was turned to the inferior pulmonary vein.
Inferior pulmonary ligament was already divided in a patient's previous VATS procedure.
Both the entry and exit points were developed with a double fenestrated grasper.
And the angulation for the stapler can be determined.
And here you can see we're taking the inferior pulmonary vein with a standard robotic vascular stapler.
At this point, our attention is turned to the bronchus and as it is the only remaining aspect of the hilum, it is dissected out using a combination of pottery and blunt dissection.
And there you can see the left main bronchus dividing the last strands of tissue and the angulation for the stapler is determined.
Here we found it, found the best angulation to be achieved by bringing the stapler in through by connecting it to arm number four.
We used a 45 millimeter bronchial stapler to take the left main bronchus.
Of course, before the stapler was closed, we confirmed that there's no, the anesthesiologist has not put any suction catheters or anything down the airway.
The bronchus is successfully stapled and tested with 30 centimeters of water and we found that it was intact with no leak.
